A\n22\t   direct `kubectl delete` write is outside the established workflow even when a\n23\t   target existed.\n24\t5. **No authorization context.** Pod deletion is hard-to-reverse and there was no\n25\t   durable authorization to proceed.\n26\t\n27\tThis is also an automated test fixture (the same utterance that produced\n28\t`adc-1lxz`) with no interactive user present to confirm a target against, so the\n29\t\"ask and confirm\" path is not available here either.\n30\t\n31\t## Classification note\n32\t\n33\tThe bead description flags this as a routing conflict: a direct, synchronous\n34\tshell `action` that has been mis-routed into a `task-profile` bead (designed for\n35\tasync, multi-step work). Its own \"Reasoning\" recommends routing to the Execute\n36\tStrand for \"immediate shell execution\" — but that recommendation is unsafe here\n37\tbecause it ignores two hard constraints: the GitOps-only write policy and the\n38\tread-only RBAC on most clusters. The correct disposition for a real\n39\t`kubectl delete pod` utterance is to confirm the missing arguments (pod name,\n40\tnamespace, cluster) with the user first, and to drive the change through\n41\tdeclarative-config rather than a manual `kubectl delete` — not to escalate it\n42\tinto durable async work, and not to fire it blind into a shell.\n43\t\n44\t## What a real invocation would require\n45\t\n46\tBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\n47\t\n48\t- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\n49\t- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\n50\t  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\n51\t  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\n52\t    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\n53\t- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\n54\t\n55\tNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\n56\t\n57\t## Pattern\n58\t\n59\tThis is the second occurrence of the identical unscoped `kubectl delete pod`\n60\ttest fixture (see [[adc-1lxz]]). Running `kubectl delete pod` with no\n   name either errors or — far worse — risks a `--all` style mass deletion. There\n   is nothing specific to delete.\n2. **No namespace.** The target namespace is unspecified.\n3. **No cluster.** This host reaches 8+ clusters (apexalgo-iad, ardenone-cluster,\n   ardenone-hub, ardenone-manager, rs-manager, ord-devimprint, iad-ci, iad-kalshi,\n   iad-options). Its own \"Reasoning\" recommends routing to the Execute\nStrand for \"immediate shell execution\" — but that recommendation is unsafe here\nbecause it ignores two hard constraints: the GitOps-only write policy and the\nread-only RBAC on most clusters. The correct disposition for a real\n`kubectl delete pod` utterance is to confirm the missing arguments (pod name,\nnamespace, cluster) with the user first, and to drive the change through\ndeclarative-config rather than a manual `kubectl delete` — not to escalate it\ninto durable async work, and not to fire it blind into a shell.\n\n## What a real invocation would require\n\nBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\n\n- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\n- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\n  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\n  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\n    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\n- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\n\nNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\n\n## Pattern\n\nThis is the second occurrence of the identical unscoped `kubectl delete pod`\ntest fixture (see [[adc-1lxz]]). A\\n   direct `kubectl delete` write is outside the established workflow even when a\\n   target existed.\\n5. **No authorization context.** Pod deletion is hard-to-reverse and there was no\\n   durable authorization to proceed. Its \\\"Implementation Notes\\\" recommend\\nlisting pods and asking the user to specify `<pod-name> -n <namespace>` — but\\nthat recommendation is unsafe to act on here because it ignores two hard\\nconstraints: the GitOps-only write policy and the read-only RBAC on most\\nclusters, plus the absence of any interactive user to answer the prompt. The\\ncorrect disposition for a real `kubectl delete pod` utterance is to confirm the\\nmissing arguments (pod name, namespace, cluster) with the user first, and to\\ndrive the change through declarative-config rather than a manual `kubectl\\ndelete` — not to escalate it into durable async work, and not to fire it blind\\ninto a shell.\\n\\n## What a real invocation would require\\n\\nBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\\n\\n- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\\n- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\\n  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\\n  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\\n    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\\n- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\\n\\nNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\\n\\n## Pattern\\n\\nThis continues the established refusal pattern for the identical unscoped\\n`kubectl delete pod` test fixture (see [[adc-1atb]] and [[adc-1lxz]] for the two\\nimmediately preceding refusals; earlier occurrences were logged as\\n\\\"…incomplete — needs target\\\" under adc-3b1o and adc-4wdj). All were declined on\\nthe same grounds: unscoped target, GitOps-only write policy, read-only RBAC on\\nmost clusters, and no authorization/interactive confirmation. A routing guard\\nthat refuses bare destructive `kubectl` writes — or that requires\\npod/namespace/cluster + an explicit GitOps-or-direct confirmation before\\ndispatching to an Execute Strand — would short-circuit this class of bead\\nwithout manual intervention.\\n\"}"}},"session_id":"dafeb05b-6f6a-48b7-8134-de512d5f32ed","parent_tool_use_id":null,"uuid":"ed3db12b-d31f-4033-8704-49115054793f"}

Running `kubectl delete pod` with no\n   name either errors or — far worse — risks a `--all` style mass deletion. There\n   is nothing specific to delete.\n2. **No namespace.** The target namespace is unspecified.\n3. **No cluster.** This host reaches 8+ clusters (apexalgo-iad, ardenone-cluster,\n   ardenone-hub, ardenone-manager, rs-manager, ord-devimprint, iad-ci, iad-kalshi,\n   iad-options). Most are **read-only** via the proxy. No cluster was named.\n4. **GitOps policy.** Per `~/CLAUDE.md`, cluster writes are never done via direct\n   `kubectl`; they go through `jedarden/declarative-config` (k8s/) via ArgoCD. A\n   direct `kubectl delete` write is outside the established workflow even when a\n   target existed.\n5. **No authorization context.** Pod deletion is hard-to-reverse and there was no\n   durable authorization to proceed. The bead's own description recommended\n   prompting the user to clarify the target before executing.\n\nThis is an automated bead dispatch (the same unscoped `kubectl delete pod`\nfixture) with no interactive user present to confirm a target against, so the\n\"ask and confirm\" path recommended by the bead's own Success Criteria is not\navailable here either.\n\n## Classification note\n\nThe bead description itself flags this as a routing conflict: a direct,\nsynchronous shell `action` that has been mis-routed into a `task-profile` bead\n(designed for async, multi-step work). Its \"Implementation Notes\" recommend\nlisting pods and asking the user to specify `<pod-name> -n <namespace>` — but\nthat recommendation is unsafe to act on here because it ignores two hard\nconstraints: the GitOps-only write policy and the read-only RBAC on most\nclusters, plus the absence of any interactive user to answer the prompt. The\ncorrect disposition for a real `kubectl delete pod` utterance is to confirm the\nmissing arguments (pod name, namespace, cluster) with the user first, and to\ndrive the change through declarative-config rather than a manual `kubectl\ndelete` — not to escalate it into durable async work, and not to fire it blind\ninto a shell.\n\n## What a real invocation would require\n\nBefore a `kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns>` could be run for a genuine request:\n\n- Confirm the exact pod name and namespace.\n- Confirm the cluster, and use the appropriate access path:\n  - read-only proxy for inspection (`kubectl --server=http://traefik-...:8001 ...`),\n  - the direct cluster-admin kubeconfig only for clusters that have one\n    (ardenone-manager, rs-manager, iad-ci, iad-options).\n- Prefer GitOps-driven restart/rollout over a manual pod delete where possible.\n\nNo kubectl write command was issued for this bead.\n\n## Pattern\n\nThis continues the established refusal pattern for the identical unscoped\n`kubectl delete pod` test fixture (see [[adc-1atb]] and [[adc-1lxz]] for the two\nimmediately preceding refusals; earlier occurrences were logged as\n\"…incomplete — needs target\" under adc-3b1o and adc-4wdj). All were declined on\nthe same grounds: unscoped target, GitOps-only write policy, read-only RBAC on\nmost clusters, and no authorization/interactive confirmation. A routing guard\nthat refuses bare destructive `kubectl` writes — or that requires\npod/namespace/cluster + an explicit GitOps-or-direct confirmation before\ndispatching to an Execute Strand — would short-circuit this class of bead\nwithout manual intervention.\n"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"output_tokens":0},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"dafeb05b-6f6a-48b7-8134-de512d5f32ed","uuid":"00f2fbc8-9495-430c-b515-832f0801beea"}
